title: Devilled kidneys
subtitle: Lambs' kidneys cooked quickly in mustard, Worcestershire, and cayenne
excerpt: Halved lambs' kidneys sautéed over very high heat in a pungent sauce of English mustard, Worcestershire sauce, and cayenne, served on toast. A proper Victorian breakfast dish that takes 10 minutes from start to table and demands nothing of the cook except heat and speed.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: devilled kidneys, recipe 746. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: devilled kidney on toast.

content.text: Three rules for devilled kidneys: the pan must be very hot, the kidneys must be fresh, and the whole thing takes no more than 10 minutes. Overcooked kidneys turn rubbery and develop a bitter flavour; cooked quickly over fierce heat they stay tender with a slight resistance at the centre. Split them open and remove the core of white sinew before cooking, this is not optional.

The devilling mixture is built from mustard, Worcestershire, and cayenne. The proportions here give a sharp, hot result. If you want it milder, reduce the cayenne. The kidneys are served on thick toast, which soaks up the sauce.
Halve each of the {{lamb-kidneys}} kidneys lengthways. Using a small knife or scissors, cut away the white sinew core. Pat the kidneys dry with kitchen paper and season with salt and pepper.

Heat a frying pan over very high heat until it begins to smoke. Add the {{unsalted-butter}} of butter and, when it foams, add the kidneys cut-side down. Cook without moving for 2 minutes until browned. Turn and cook for a further 1 to 2 minutes. They should feel slightly firm when pressed, not soft. Add the {{english-mustard}} of English mustard, {{worcestershire-sauce}} of Worcestershire sauce, and a good pinch of cayenne. Swirl the pan to coat the kidneys in the sauce. Cook for 30 seconds, then remove from the heat immediately.
Pile the kidneys and sauce onto the toast. Scatter with {{parsley-flat}} of parsley and serve immediately.

items.fix: Pat kidneys dry before cooking; keep the total cooking time to 4 to 5 minutes over very high heat; they should be cooked through but still giving when pressed
items.cause: Overcooked or not dried before cooking

items.fix: Cut away the entire white core before cooking; buy kidneys the day you plan to use them
items.cause: The sinew core was not fully removed, or the kidneys were not fresh
items.symptom: Kidneys have a strong, bitter smell

items.fix: Heat the pan until it smokes before adding the butter; cook in two batches if the pan is not large enough to hold the kidneys in a single layer
items.cause: Pan not hot enough, or too many kidneys crowded in at once
items.symptom: Pan has not browned the kidneys. They have steamed instead

items.fix: Remove the pan from the heat before adding the sauce ingredients; a little residual butter is enough to keep it fluid
items.cause: Added the mustard and Worcestershire to a completely dry pan after all the butter evaporated
items.symptom: Sauce has seized and stuck to the pan

content.text: Devilled kidneys were a standard breakfast dish on Victorian and Edwardian upper-middle-class tables, served alongside kippers, kedgeree, and cold cuts. The devilling technique applies hot seasonings to meat or fish and cooks it briefly over fierce heat. It appears throughout British cookery writing from the 1820s onward. The dish fell out of fashion as breakfast shrank, but it was never lost. It is worth reviving.
